evidence_quotes.quote: In todays digital market, buying a video game often feels more like renting it under uncertain terms.
consumers rights must be protected against the blanket power of restrictive EULAs.

paragraphs.text: In todays digital market, buying a video game often feels more like renting it under uncertain terms. A growing number of titles are locked behind online connections, and when publishers decide to pull the plug, players are left with nothing but broken software. Something that was once purchased and enjoyed simply stops existing in any meaningful way. No one objects to companies ending updates or support after a reasonable timethat is natural. But there is a fundamental difference between halting new development and deliberately shutting down access to something people already paid for. In the physical world, a book or a record doesnt vanish because the publisher moved on. Yet in the digital world, this unfair practice has become normal. Whats missing are safeguards. When servers close, there should always be a fallback: an offline mode, a patch enabling local hosting, or permission for communities to maintain the game. Denying all of these options amounts to destroying consumer property under the guise of license terms. The Digital Fairness Act offers a chance to put an end to this imbalance. I call on policymakers to ensure that purchased digital products cannot be made completely unusable by unilateral corporate decisions. Games should remain functional in some form, and consumers rights must be protected against the blanket power of restrictive EULAs. Fairness, durability, and usability should not stop at the edge of the digital worldthey must apply there as well.
